name: Icosahedral Al-Mn
institution: National Bureau of Standards, Gaithersburg, Maryland, USA
tiling_analog: 3D icosahedral Penrose tiling (oblate and prolate rhombohedra)
space_group_notes: Quasiperiodic, no space group in classical sense; 6D superspace group Pa͛35̅ (No. 217 analog)
preparation_method: rapid solidification (melt spinning), cooling rate ~10^6 K/s

stability_range.note: Metastable; transforms to periodic Al6Mn on annealing above ~350 C

x_ray_diffraction.peak_positions_ratio: irrational, tau-based (powers of golden ratio phi=1.618...)

visual_profile: Silver metallic with subtle 5-fold facets; rapidly quenched thin ribbons
notes: First discovered quasicrystal (April 8, 1982, published 1984); showed forbidden icosahedral symmetry. Shechtman received Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2011. Metastable phase; transforms to periodic Al6Mn on annealing.

name: Stable Icosahedral Al-Cu-Fe
space_group_notes: Thermodynamically stable; 6D superspace group Fm3͛5̅ (No. 225 analog)
preparation_method: conventional casting and annealing; also single crystal growth by Czochralski-like method

stability_range.note: Stable single-phase below solidus; Al-Cu-Fe phase diagram has narrow stability field

visual_profile: Bright silver-gold metallic; forms large faceted single-grain pieces
notes: First thermodynamically STABLE icosahedral quasicrystal. Landmark discovery by Tsai (1987). Very low friction coefficient (~0.05, comparable to PTFE/Teflon). Large single crystals possible. Used in non-stick coating and low-friction surface research.

name: Icosahedral Ti-Zr-Ni
space_group_notes: Metastable to moderately stable; transforms on prolonged annealing
preparation_method: rapid solidification, arc melting followed by quenching

stability_range.note: Transforms to periodic Ti2Ni and related phases on annealing

hydrogen_storage.note: Ti-Zr-Ni QC investigated for hydrogen storage applications; absorbs H2 reversibly

visual_profile: Dull grey metallic; typical of Ti-Zr alloys
notes: Ti-Zr-Ni system notable for hydrogen storage properties. Related to work by Elser and Henley on icosahedral Frank-Kasper phases. Used in studies connecting quasicrystal structure to hydrogen absorption.

name: Binary Icosahedral Cd-Yb
institution: National Institute for Materials Science (NIMS), Tsukuba, Japan
space_group_notes: Thermodynamically stable binary quasicrystal; first binary icosahedral QC
preparation_method: melt growth, slow cooling; large single crystals up to mm size

stability_range.note: Stable binary; Cd vapor pressure high, handle with care above 400C

x_ray_diffraction.pattern_type: icosahedral point group diffraction; first fully solved icosahedral structure

structural_clusters.note: Five concentric icosahedral shells; first icosahedral QC with solved atomic structure

visual_profile: Bright silver-white metallic; large single grains with icosahedral facets visible
notes: First binary (two-element) icosahedral quasicrystal. Nobel-worthy contribution by Tsai (2000). Only two elements enabled complete atomic structure solution. Revealed concentric icosahedral shell motif (Tsai cluster). Published in Nature 408, 537 (2000).

name: Icosahedral Al-Pd-Mn
space_group_notes: Thermodynamically stable; large single crystals possible (cm scale); face-centered icosahedral (FCI) type
preparation_method: Bridgman growth, slow cooling; single crystals up to 1 cm grown by solution growth

stability_range.note: Stable; used for fundamental physics measurements due to large single crystal availability

x_ray_diffraction.pattern_type: icosahedral FCI type; extremely sharp peaks in perfect single grains

transport_properties.note: Conductivity increases with disorder and with temperature — opposite of normal metals. Al-Pd-Mn was key material for establishing unusual transport in QC.

surface_science.five_fold_surface: 5-fold symmetric surface cleavage plane shows quasiperiodic terrace pattern in STM

visual_profile: Mirror-bright silver metallic; perfect single crystals show icosahedral facets
notes: Workhorse material for QC physics. Large single crystals enable anisotropy measurements. 5-fold surface imaged by STM showing quasiperiodic atomic terraces. Exhibits inverse Matthiessen rule (conductivity increases with disorder). Critical material for understanding QC electronic properties.

name: Icosahedral Al-Li-Cu
space_group_notes: Stable in certain Al-Li-Cu compositions; body-centered icosahedral (BCI) type
preparation_method: conventional casting of Al-Li-Cu alloys; QC phase appears on solidification

stability_range.note: Stable in Al-Li-Cu; low density due to Li content makes it interesting for aerospace

lightweight_properties.note: Low density (2.39 g/cm3) due to lithium content makes Al-Li-Cu QC interesting for aerospace lightweight applications
lightweight_properties.density_comparison: Comparable to standard Al alloys (~2.7 g/cm3) but lighter due to Li

visual_profile: Light grey-silver; lower density than typical Al-Cu-Fe or Al-Pd-Mn QC
notes: First known icosahedral quasicrystal in a commercially relevant alloy system (Al-Li). Discovered in 1986 by Ball and Lloyd at Alcan. Low density due to Li makes it of interest for lightweight structural applications. BCI (body-centered icosahedral) type structure.

name: Icosahedral Zn-Mg-Ho (RE-containing)
discoverer: A.-P. Tsai, A. Niikura, A. Inoue, T. Masumoto
space_group_notes: Stable icosahedral QC; Zn-Mg-RE system with various RE (Y, Ho, Er, Tb, Dy, Nd, Sm, Gd, Tb)
preparation_method: conventional casting; Zn-Mg-RE alloys form QC phase on equilibrium solidification

stability_range.note: Stable; RE choice affects magnetic properties

magnetic_properties.magnetic_moment_RE: Ho3+ has large magnetic moment (10.6 mu_B)
magnetic_properties.note: Rare-earth moments on quasiperiodic lattice show spin-glass freezing. No long-range magnetic order. Frustration due to quasiperiodic geometry.

visual_profile: Grey-silver metallic; powder or cast ingot form; RE substitution gives same visual appearance
notes: Zn-Mg-Ho is part of a series of Zn-Mg-RE (RE = rare earth) stable icosahedral QC. Ho, Y, Er, Dy substitutions all produce stable icosahedral phase. Magnetic RE atoms (Ho, Er, Dy) show spin-glass behavior at low T due to quasiperiodic frustration. Important for studies of magnetism in aperiodic lattices.

name: Decagonal Al-Co-Ni
tiling_analog: 2D Penrose tiling (P2 or P3) stacked periodically along 10-fold axis
space_group_notes: Quasiperiodic in 2D (within basal plane), periodic along 10-fold axis; period along axis ~0.4 nm (2-layer) or ~0.8 nm (4-layer) depending on composition
preparation_method: conventional casting, zone melting; single crystal growth by Czochralski method

stability_range.note: Stable single phase in Al-rich corner of Al-Co-Ni phase diagram

x_ray_diffraction.peak_positions_ratio: irrational, tau-based in basal plane; rational (integer) along 10-fold axis
x_ray_diffraction.pattern_type: decagonal 10/mmm diffraction; sharp 10-fold pattern in [001] zone axis
x_ray_diffraction.indexing: 5+1 dimensional indexing: (h1,h2,h3,h4,h5) in plane + l along axis

anisotropy.note: Decagonal QC is anisotropic: properties differ between in-plane and out-of-plane directions, unlike icosahedral QC which is isotropic

phasons.phason_strain: observable in diffraction as diffuse scattering
phasons.note: Phasons in decagonal QC exist only in the 2D quasiperiodic plane, not along the periodic axis

visual_profile: Silver-grey metallic; anisotropic single crystals show different faces along periodic vs. quasiperiodic directions
notes: Most studied decagonal (10-fold) quasicrystal. Quasiperiodic in 2D (Penrose-like) + periodic in 3rd direction. Anisotropic physical properties. Large single crystals available. Al72Co8Ni20 and Al70Co15Ni15 are two studied compositions. High melting point (1148 C) allows high-T studies.
